# ---- R chunk ----
# ============================================================
# 0. Packages
# ============================================================
# Luyi note: I moved all package loading to the top so the file can knit from start to finish.
# If a package is missing, run install.packages("package_name") once before knitting.

required_packages <- c(
  "tidyverse",   # data cleaning, reshaping, plotting
  "readxl",      # reading Excel files
  "janitor",     # cleaning variable names and removing empty rows
  "psych",       # descriptive statistics
  "lme4",        # linear mixed-effects models
  "lmerTest",    # Satterthwaite df and Type III tests for lmer models
  "emmeans",     # estimated marginal means and planned contrasts
  "effectsize",  # effect-size helpers
  "performance", # model diagnostics
  "broom.mixed", # tidy model output
  "car",         # optional Levene/outlier tools if needed
  "rstatix",     # outlier and assumption helpers
  "afex",        # optional repeated-measures ANOVA sensitivity check
  "gt",          # publication-style tables
  "gtsummary",   # demographic summary tables
  "here"         # reproducible file paths
)

missing_packages <- required_packages[!required_packages %in% rownames(installed.packages())]
if (length(missing_packages) > 0) {
  stop(
    paste0(
      "Please install the following packages before knitting: ",
      paste(missing_packages, collapse = ", ")
    )
  )
}

invisible(lapply(required_packages, library, character.only = TRUE))

# Type III tests are easiest to interpret when using sum-to-zero contrasts.
# Luyi note: This also keeps the output closer to what we usually report for factorial models.
options(contrasts = c("contr.sum", "contr.poly"))
emmeans::emm_options(lmer.df = "satterthwaite")

# Create output folders so tables and figures are saved consistently.
dir.create("outputs", showWarnings = FALSE)
dir.create("outputs/tables", recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)
dir.create("outputs/figures", recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)

# ---- R chunk ----
# ============================================================
# 1. Import raw data
# ============================================================
# Luyi note: I changed the import to local-first because the final submission should run
# with the data file we attach. The GitHub link is kept only as a backup.

local_paths <- c(
  here::here("data", "Raw Data.xlsx"),
  here::here("Raw Data.xlsx"),
  "data/Raw Data.xlsx",
  "Raw Data.xlsx"
)

existing_path <- local_paths[file.exists(local_paths)][1]

github_raw_url <- "https://github.com/ZeddyCraft/HUDM-5123-Final-Project/raw/refs/heads/main/Raw%20Data.xlsx"

if (!is.na(existing_path)) {
  data_raw <- readxl::read_excel(existing_path)
  message("Data imported from local file: ", existing_path)
} else {
  # Backup only. If this fails, place Raw Data.xlsx in the project folder or data/ folder.
  temp_file <- tempfile(fileext = ".xlsx")
  download.file(github_raw_url, destfile = temp_file, mode = "wb", quiet = TRUE)
  data_raw <- readxl::read_excel(temp_file)
  message("Data imported from GitHub backup URL.")
}

head(data_raw)

# ---- R chunk ----
# ============================================================
# 2. Clean variable names, remove blank rows, create ID, rename variables
# ============================================================
# Luyi note: This section documents the exact cleaning steps that our Methods section says we used.

# Clean variable names and remove completely empty rows.
data <- data_raw %>%
  janitor::clean_names() %>%
  janitor::remove_empty(which = "rows") %>%
  mutate(id = row_number()) %>%
  relocate(id)

# Check that the raw variables needed for the PSS analysis exist after clean_names().
required_raw_variables <- c(
  "group_a_mindfulness_b_wait_list",
  "gender_1_male_2_female",
  "age",
  "pss_baseline",
  "pss_day_10",
  "pss_day_30"
)

missing_raw_variables <- setdiff(required_raw_variables, names(data))
if (length(missing_raw_variables) > 0) {
  stop(
    paste0(
      "These required variables are missing after clean_names(): ",
      paste(missing_raw_variables, collapse = ", ")
    )
  )
}

# Rename variables used in the confirmatory PSS analysis.
# I am keeping SWLS/RWS names too, but they are not used as confirmatory outcomes below.
data <- data %>%
  rename(
    group = group_a_mindfulness_b_wait_list,
    gender = gender_1_male_2_female,

    swls_b = swls_baseline,
    swls_d10 = swls_day_10,
    swls_d30 = swls_day_30,

    pss_b = pss_baseline,
    pss_d10 = pss_day_10,
    pss_d30 = pss_day_30,

    rws_b = rws_baseline,
    rws_d10 = rws_day_10,
    rws_d30 = rws_day_30
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    group = factor(
      group,
      levels = c("A", "B"),
      labels = c("Mindfulness", "Wait-list")
    ),
    gender = factor(
      gender,
      levels = c(1, 2),
      labels = c("Male", "Female")
    )
  )

# Quick checks.
glimpse(data)
table(data$group, useNA = "ifany")
table(data$gender, useNA = "ifany")

# ---- R chunk ----
# ============================================================
# 3. Codebook
# ============================================================
# Luyi note: The draft had a note asking for a reproducible codebook, so I added one here.

codebook <- tibble::tribble(
  ~variable, ~role_in_analysis, ~type, ~coding_or_range, ~description,
  "id", "Participant identifier", "Integer", "1 to N", "Anonymous participant ID created after removing empty rows.",
  "group", "Between-subjects predictor", "Factor", "Mindfulness; Wait-list", "Randomized assignment condition.",
  "gender", "Descriptive variable", "Factor", "Male; Female", "Participant gender as coded in the dataset.",
  "age", "Descriptive variable", "Numeric", "Years", "Participant age in years.",
  "pss_b", "Outcome at baseline", "Numeric", "0-40", "Perceived Stress Scale score at baseline; higher scores indicate greater perceived stress.",
  "pss_d10", "Outcome at Day 10", "Numeric", "0-40", "Perceived Stress Scale score at Day 10.",
  "pss_d30", "Outcome at Day 30", "Numeric", "0-40", "Perceived Stress Scale score at Day 30.",
  "time_num", "Within-subjects predictor for replication model", "Numeric", "0, 10, 30", "Assessment time coded in days since baseline.",
  "time_factor", "Within-subjects predictor for extension model", "Factor", "Baseline; Day 10; Day 30", "Assessment occasion treated categorically.",
  "pss", "Primary outcome", "Numeric", "0-40", "Long-format PSS score."
)

codebook
write_csv(codebook, "outputs/tables/codebook.csv")

# ---- R chunk ----
# ============================================================
# 4. Wide-to-long reshape for PSS
# ============================================================
# Luyi note: I am creating both time_num and time_factor because our paper says we first
# replicate the original numeric-time model and then extend it with categorical time.

pss_long <- data %>%
  select(id, group, gender, age, pss_b, pss_d10, pss_d30) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(pss_b, pss_d10, pss_d30),
    names_to = "time_raw",
    values_to = "pss"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    time_factor = case_when(
      time_raw == "pss_b" ~ "Baseline",
      time_raw == "pss_d10" ~ "Day 10",
      time_raw == "pss_d30" ~ "Day 30",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    time_factor = factor(
      time_factor,
      levels = c("Baseline", "Day 10", "Day 30")
    ),
    time_num = case_when(
      time_raw == "pss_b" ~ 0,
      time_raw == "pss_d10" ~ 10,
      time_raw == "pss_d30" ~ 30,
      TRUE ~ NA_real_
    )
  ) %>%
  arrange(id, time_num)

head(pss_long, 10)
glimpse(pss_long)

# ---- R chunk ----
# ============================================================
# 5. Validate PSS range and summarize missingness
# ============================================================
# Luyi note: This makes explicit that PSS is in the valid 0-40 range.

pss_range_check <- pss_long %>%
  summarise(
    min_pss = min(pss, na.rm = TRUE),
    max_pss = max(pss, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_below_0 = sum(pss < 0, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_above_40 = sum(pss > 40, na.rm = TRUE)
  )

pss_range_check

if (any(pss_long$pss < 0 | pss_long$pss > 40, na.rm = TRUE)) {
  stop("Some PSS values are outside the valid 0-40 range.")
}

# Missingness by randomized condition and assessment occasion.
pss_missing_table <- pss_long %>%
  group_by(group, time_factor) %>%
  summarise(
    n_total = n(),
    n_observed = sum(!is.na(pss)),
    n_missing = sum(is.na(pss)),
    prop_missing = n_missing / n_total,
    .groups = "drop"
  )

pss_missing_table
write_csv(pss_missing_table, "outputs/tables/pss_missing_table.csv")

# Complete-case count by group.
pss_complete_cases <- pss_long %>%
  group_by(id, group) %>%
  summarise(
    n_observed = sum(!is.na(pss)),
    complete_case = n_observed == 3,
    .groups = "drop"
  )

pss_complete_case_table <- pss_complete_cases %>%
  group_by(group, complete_case) %>%
  summarise(n = n(), .groups = "drop")

pss_complete_case_table
write_csv(pss_complete_case_table, "outputs/tables/pss_complete_case_table.csv")

# Basic attrition descriptives.
# Luyi note: I am not running hypothesis tests for baseline balance here; I am just describing completers vs non-completers.
data_attrition <- data %>%
  left_join(
    pss_complete_cases %>% select(id, complete_case),
    by = "id"
  )

attrition_descriptives <- data_attrition %>%
  group_by(group, complete_case) %>%
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    age_mean = mean(age, na.rm = TRUE),
    age_sd = sd(age, na.rm = TRUE),
    pss_baseline_mean = mean(pss_b, na.rm = TRUE),
    pss_baseline_sd = sd(pss_b, na.rm = TRUE),
    male_n = sum(gender == "Male", na.rm = TRUE),
    female_n = sum(gender == "Female", na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

attrition_descriptives
write_csv(attrition_descriptives, "outputs/tables/attrition_descriptives.csv")

# ---- R chunk ----
# ============================================================
# 6. Descriptive statistics for demographics and PSS
# ============================================================
# Luyi note: The rubric requires descriptive statistics. I split demographics and PSS
# descriptives so the tables stay readable.

# Demographics by randomized condition.
demographic_table <- data %>%
  group_by(group) %>%
  summarise(
    n_participants = n(),
    age_mean = mean(age, na.rm = TRUE),
    age_sd = sd(age, na.rm = TRUE),
    age_min = min(age, na.rm = TRUE),
    age_max = max(age, na.rm = TRUE),
    male_n = sum(gender == "Male", na.rm = TRUE),
    male_percent = mean(gender == "Male", na.rm = TRUE) * 100,
    female_n = sum(gender == "Female", na.rm = TRUE),
    female_percent = mean(gender == "Female", na.rm = TRUE) * 100,
    .groups = "drop"
  )

demographic_table
write_csv(demographic_table, "outputs/tables/demographic_table.csv")

# PSS descriptives by group and time.
pss_descriptive_table <- pss_long %>%
  group_by(group, time_factor) %>%
  summarise(
    n_total = n(),
    n_observed = sum(!is.na(pss)),
    n_missing = sum(is.na(pss)),
    mean = mean(pss, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd = sd(pss, na.rm = TRUE),
    min = min(pss, na.rm = TRUE),
    max = max(pss, na.rm = TRUE),
    se = sd / sqrt(n_observed),
    ci_lower = if_else(n_observed > 1, mean - qt(.975, df = n_observed - 1) * se, NA_real_),
    ci_upper = if_else(n_observed > 1, mean + qt(.975, df = n_observed - 1) * se, NA_real_),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

pss_descriptive_table
write_csv(pss_descriptive_table, "outputs/tables/pss_descriptive_table.csv")

# APA-style display tables.
demographic_gt <- demographic_table %>%
  gt() %>%
  tab_header(
    title = md("**Demographic Characteristics by Randomized Condition**")
  ) %>%
  fmt_number(
    columns = c(age_mean, age_sd, age_min, age_max, male_percent, female_percent),
    decimals = 2
  )

pss_descriptive_gt <- pss_descriptive_table %>%
  gt() %>%
  tab_header(
    title = md("**PSS Descriptive Statistics by Condition and Time**")
  ) %>%
  fmt_number(
    columns = c(mean, sd, min, max, se, ci_lower, ci_upper),
    decimals = 2
  )

demographic_gt
pss_descriptive_gt

# ---- R chunk ----
# ============================================================
# 7A. Helper function for fitting LMMs
# ============================================================
# Luyi note: I wrote this helper because the numeric-time random-slope model may have
# convergence or singular-fit issues. This lets us document which model works best.

fit_lmer_safely <- function(formula, data, reml = FALSE, label = "") {
  fit <- tryCatch(
    lmer(
      formula = formula,
      data = data,
      REML = reml,
      na.action = na.exclude,
      control = lmerControl(
        optimizer = "bobyqa",
        optCtrl = list(maxfun = 100000)
      )
    ),
    error = function(e) {
      message("Model failed: ", label, " | ", e$message)
      return(NULL)
    }
  )
  return(fit)
}

model_status <- function(fit, label, formula_text) {
  if (is.null(fit)) {
    return(tibble(
      model = label,
      formula = formula_text,
      fitted = FALSE,
      singular = NA,
      nobs = NA_integer_,
      logLik = NA_real_,
      AIC = NA_real_,
      BIC = NA_real_
    ))
  }

  tibble(
    model = label,
    formula = formula_text,
    fitted = TRUE,
    singular = isSingular(fit, tol = 1e-4),
    nobs = nobs(fit),
    logLik = as.numeric(logLik(fit)),
    AIC = AIC(fit),
    BIC = BIC(fit)
  )
}

# ---- R chunk ----
# ============================================================
# 7B. Numeric-time replication model
# ============================================================
# Luyi note: This is the replication-oriented model because the original paper treated time as 0, 10, 30 days.
# We first try the random-slope model. If it is unstable, the random-intercept model is still available.

pss_lmm_num_full_ml <- fit_lmer_safely(
  pss ~ group * time_num + (1 + time_num | id),
  data = pss_long,
  reml = FALSE,
  label = "numeric full random slope ML"
)

pss_lmm_num_uncor_ml <- fit_lmer_safely(
  pss ~ group * time_num + (1 + time_num || id),
  data = pss_long,
  reml = FALSE,
  label = "numeric uncorrelated random slope ML"
)

pss_lmm_num_ri_ml <- fit_lmer_safely(
  pss ~ group * time_num + (1 | id),
  data = pss_long,
  reml = FALSE,
  label = "numeric random-intercept ML"
)

numeric_model_status <- bind_rows(
  model_status(pss_lmm_num_full_ml, "Numeric time: random intercept + random slope", "pss ~ group * time_num + (1 + time_num | id)"),
  model_status(pss_lmm_num_uncor_ml, "Numeric time: uncorrelated random slope", "pss ~ group * time_num + (1 + time_num || id)"),
  model_status(pss_lmm_num_ri_ml, "Numeric time: random intercept only", "pss ~ group * time_num + (1 | id)")
)

numeric_model_status
write_csv(numeric_model_status, "outputs/tables/numeric_model_status.csv")

# Choose the first fitted, non-singular numeric model; if all are singular, use the random-intercept model.
numeric_fits <- list(
  full_random_slope = pss_lmm_num_full_ml,
  uncorrelated_random_slope = pss_lmm_num_uncor_ml,
  random_intercept = pss_lmm_num_ri_ml
)

numeric_fits <- numeric_fits[!vapply(numeric_fits, is.null, logical(1))]
non_singular_numeric <- numeric_fits[!vapply(numeric_fits, isSingular, logical(1), tol = 1e-4)]

if (length(non_singular_numeric) > 0) {
  pss_lmm_num_ml <- non_singular_numeric[[1]]
  retained_numeric_model_name <- names(non_singular_numeric)[1]
} else {
  pss_lmm_num_ml <- pss_lmm_num_ri_ml
  retained_numeric_model_name <- "random_intercept"
}

retained_numeric_model_name
summary(pss_lmm_num_ml)
anova(pss_lmm_num_ml, type = 3, ddf = "Satterthwaite")

# ---- R chunk ----
# ============================================================
# 7C. Categorical-time extension model: primary model
# ============================================================
# Luyi note: This is the main model for our paper because it does not force the Day 0-Day 10
# and Day 10-Day 30 changes to follow one straight linear trend.

# ML version for model comparison.
pss_lmm_cat_ml <- lmer(
  pss ~ group * time_factor + (1 | id),
  data = pss_long,
  REML = FALSE,
  na.action = na.exclude,
  control = lmerControl(
    optimizer = "bobyqa",
    optCtrl = list(maxfun = 100000)
  )
)

# REML version for final estimates and planned contrasts.
pss_lmm_cat <- lmer(
  pss ~ group * time_factor + (1 | id),
  data = pss_long,
  REML = TRUE,
  na.action = na.exclude,
  control = lmerControl(
    optimizer = "bobyqa",
    optCtrl = list(maxfun = 100000)
  )
)

summary(pss_lmm_cat)
cat_lmm_tests <- anova(pss_lmm_cat, type = 3, ddf = "Satterthwaite")
cat_lmm_tests

cat_model_status <- tibble(
  model = "Categorical time: random intercept only",
  formula = "pss ~ group * time_factor + (1 | id)",
  REML = TRUE,
  singular = isSingular(pss_lmm_cat, tol = 1e-4),
  nobs = nobs(pss_lmm_cat),
  AIC = AIC(pss_lmm_cat),
  BIC = BIC(pss_lmm_cat)
)

cat_model_status
write_csv(cat_model_status, "outputs/tables/categorical_model_status.csv")

# Fixed-effect estimates and omnibus fixed-effect tests.
cat_fixed_effects_table <- broom.mixed::tidy(
  pss_lmm_cat,
  effects = "fixed",
  conf.int = TRUE
)

cat_tests_table <- cat_lmm_tests %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  rownames_to_column("effect") %>%
  as_tibble()

cat_fixed_effects_table
cat_tests_table

write_csv(cat_fixed_effects_table, "outputs/tables/categorical_lmm_fixed_effects.csv")
write_csv(cat_tests_table, "outputs/tables/categorical_lmm_tests.csv")

# ---- R chunk ----
# ============================================================
# 7D. Compare numeric-time and categorical-time models
# ============================================================
# Luyi note: For a fair ML comparison, both models below use the same random-effects structure: (1 | id).
# The full random-slope numeric model above is useful for replication, but it should not be compared
# directly with a random-intercept categorical model.

model_comparison_lrt <- anova(pss_lmm_num_ri_ml, pss_lmm_cat_ml)
model_comparison_lrt

model_comparison_aic_bic <- bind_rows(
  model_status(pss_lmm_num_ri_ml, "Numeric time RI, ML", "pss ~ group * time_num + (1 | id)"),
  model_status(pss_lmm_cat_ml, "Categorical time RI, ML", "pss ~ group * time_factor + (1 | id)")
)

model_comparison_aic_bic
write_csv(as.data.frame(model_comparison_lrt), "outputs/tables/model_comparison_lrt.csv")
write_csv(model_comparison_aic_bic, "outputs/tables/model_comparison_aic_bic.csv")

# ---- R chunk ----
# ============================================================
# 8. Planned contrasts from the categorical-time model
# ============================================================
# Luyi note: I replaced the large set of pairwise tests with the three planned difference-in-change contrasts.
# Negative estimates mean the mindfulness group had a larger reduction in PSS than the wait-list group.

pss_emm_by_group <- emmeans(pss_lmm_cat, ~ time_factor | group)
pss_emm_cells <- emmeans(pss_lmm_cat, ~ group * time_factor)

# Within-group changes first.
pss_changes <- contrast(
  pss_emm_by_group,
  method = list(
    "Baseline to Day 10" = c(-1, 1, 0),
    "Day 10 to Day 30" = c(0, -1, 1),
    "Baseline to Day 30" = c(-1, 0, 1)
  ),
  by = "group",
  name = "change_window"
)

pss_changes_summary <- summary(pss_changes, infer = TRUE) %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  as_tibble()

pss_changes_summary

# Between-condition difference in those changes.
# The comparison is Mindfulness minus Wait-list because group levels are ordered that way.
pss_difference_in_change <- pairs(
  pss_changes,
  by = "change_window",
  adjust = "holm"
)

planned_contrasts_table <- summary(pss_difference_in_change, infer = TRUE) %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  as_tibble() %>%
  mutate(
    standardized_estimate = estimate / sigma(pss_lmm_cat)
  )

planned_contrasts_table
write_csv(pss_changes_summary, "outputs/tables/pss_within_group_changes.csv")
write_csv(planned_contrasts_table, "outputs/tables/pss_difference_in_change_contrasts.csv")

planned_contrasts_gt <- planned_contrasts_table %>%
  gt() %>%
  tab_header(
    title = md("**Planned Difference-in-Change Contrasts for PSS**"),
    subtitle = "Negative estimates indicate greater PSS reduction in the mindfulness group relative to the wait-list group."
  ) %>%
  fmt_number(
    columns = where(is.numeric),
    decimals = 3
  )

planned_contrasts_gt

# ---- R chunk ----
# ============================================================
# 9. Model-estimated PSS profile plot
# ============================================================
# Luyi note: This replaces the raw mean ± SE plot. The paper says the figure should show
# model-estimated PSS means with 95% CIs, so I am using emmeans from the final LMM.

pss_emm_plot <- pss_emm_cells %>%
  as.data.frame()

p_pss_model <- ggplot(
  pss_emm_plot,
  aes(x = time_factor, y = emmean, group = group, color = group)
) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(ymin = lower.CL, ymax = upper.CL),
    width = .15
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Model-Estimated Perceived Stress Across Time",
    x = "Assessment Time",
    y = "Estimated Mean PSS Score",
    color = "Group"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)

p_pss_model

ggsave(
  filename = "outputs/figures/PSS_Model_Estimated_Figure.png",
  plot = p_pss_model,
  width = 8,
  height = 6,
  dpi = 300
)

# ---- R chunk ----
# ============================================================
# 10. Diagnostics for the final categorical-time LMM
# ============================================================
# Luyi note: These diagnostics match the LMM model. I am not treating sphericity as a primary assumption here.

# Summary diagnostic panel from performance.
# I wrapped this in try() so knitting does not stop if the optional plotting backend is unavailable.
diagnostic_panel <- try(performance::check_model(pss_lmm_cat), silent = TRUE)
diagnostic_panel

# Diagnostic dataframe based on the model frame actually used by lmer.
diag_df <- model.frame(pss_lmm_cat) %>%
  as_tibble() %>%
  mutate(
    .fitted = fitted(pss_lmm_cat),
    .resid = resid(pss_lmm_cat),
    .std_resid = as.numeric(scale(.resid))
  )

# Residual Q-Q plot.
qqnorm(diag_df$.resid)
qqline(diag_df$.resid)

# Residuals vs fitted values.
p_resid_fitted <- ggplot(diag_df, aes(x = .fitted, y = .resid)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    x = "Fitted values",
    y = "Residuals",
    title = "Residuals vs Fitted Values"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)

p_resid_fitted

ggsave(
  filename = "outputs/figures/PSS_LMM_Residuals_vs_Fitted.png",
  plot = p_resid_fitted,
  width = 8,
  height = 6,
  dpi = 300
)

# Residuals by time.
p_resid_time <- ggplot(diag_df, aes(x = time_factor, y = .resid)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(
    x = "Assessment Time",
    y = "Residuals",
    title = "Residuals by Time"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)

p_resid_time

ggsave(
  filename = "outputs/figures/PSS_LMM_Residuals_by_Time.png",
  plot = p_resid_time,
  width = 8,
  height = 6,
  dpi = 300
)

# Residuals by group.
p_resid_group <- ggplot(diag_df, aes(x = group, y = .resid)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(
    x = "Group",
    y = "Residuals",
    title = "Residuals by Group"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)

p_resid_group

ggsave(
  filename = "outputs/figures/PSS_LMM_Residuals_by_Group.png",
  plot = p_resid_group,
  width = 8,
  height = 6,
  dpi = 300
)

# Flag large standardized residuals. I am not removing them automatically.
large_residuals <- diag_df %>%
  filter(abs(.std_resid) > 3)

large_residuals
write_csv(large_residuals, "outputs/tables/large_standardized_residuals.csv")

# ---- R chunk ----
# ============================================================
# 11. Complete-case sensitivity analysis
# ============================================================
# Luyi note: The available-case LMM above is primary. This complete-case model is only a sensitivity check.

complete_ids <- pss_complete_cases %>%
  filter(complete_case) %>%
  select(id)

pss_long_complete <- pss_long %>%
  semi_join(complete_ids, by = "id")

pss_lmm_cat_complete <- lmer(
  pss ~ group * time_factor + (1 | id),
  data = pss_long_complete,
  REML = TRUE,
  na.action = na.exclude,
  control = lmerControl(
    optimizer = "bobyqa",
    optCtrl = list(maxfun = 100000)
  )
)

summary(pss_lmm_cat_complete)
complete_case_tests <- anova(pss_lmm_cat_complete, type = 3, ddf = "Satterthwaite")
complete_case_tests

pss_emm_complete <- emmeans(pss_lmm_cat_complete, ~ time_factor | group)

pss_changes_complete <- contrast(
  pss_emm_complete,
  method = list(
    "Baseline to Day 10" = c(-1, 1, 0),
    "Day 10 to Day 30" = c(0, -1, 1),
    "Baseline to Day 30" = c(-1, 0, 1)
  ),
  by = "group",
  name = "change_window"
)

pss_difference_in_change_complete <- pairs(
  pss_changes_complete,
  by = "change_window",
  adjust = "holm"
)

complete_case_contrasts_table <- summary(pss_difference_in_change_complete, infer = TRUE) %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  as_tibble() %>%
  mutate(
    standardized_estimate = estimate / sigma(pss_lmm_cat_complete)
  )

complete_case_contrasts_table
write_csv(as.data.frame(complete_case_tests), "outputs/tables/complete_case_lmm_tests.csv")
write_csv(complete_case_contrasts_table, "outputs/tables/complete_case_difference_in_change_contrasts.csv")

# ---- R chunk ----
# ============================================================
# 12. Optional repeated-measures ANOVA sensitivity check
# ============================================================
# Luyi note: I am keeping this only as a sensitivity check because repeated-measures ANOVA
# generally relies on complete cases, while our primary LMM uses all observed PSS scores.

pss_aov_sensitivity <- afex::aov_ez(
  id = "id",
  dv = "pss",
  between = "group",
  within = "time_factor",
  data = pss_long,
  type = 3,
  anova_table = list(correction = "GG", es = "ges")
)

summary(pss_aov_sensitivity)
afex::nice(pss_aov_sensitivity)

# ---- R chunk ----
# ============================================================
# 13. Session info for reproducibility
# ============================================================
# Luyi note: This helps if the professor or we need to reproduce the output later.

sessionInfo()
